source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5472__Midcontinent-Independent-System-Operator-Inc/2025-11-21_Docket No. RM26-4-000 MISO Comments to DOE proposed ANOPR.txt
overall_summary: MISO opposes mandating standard large load interconnection procedures, arguing its existing regional processes effectively interconnect large loads and that a one-size-fits-all rule would introduce delays rather than the speed to power DOE seeks. MISO recommends the Commission instead allow RTOs to continue using region-specific processes, or at most issue a policy statement with guiding principles and extend the Independent Entity Variation to any final rule.

quotes.text: the Commission should be mindful of regional differences that might limit the success of a "one size fits all" mandate.  Shifting to standardized large load interconnection procedures could nullify currently effective processes and/or delay significant work that has been undertaken and currently ongoing.
quotes.concern: Mandatory uniform procedures could undermine effective regional processes already in place.

quotes.text: shifting to a one-size-fits all approach could introduce delays to interconnecting large loads, which would be contrary to the very progress desired by the DOE and the Commission.
quotes.concern: Standardization would slow, not accelerate, large load interconnections.

quotes.text: Fundamentally, MISO questions whether standardized large load interconnection procedures will result in the "speed to power" that is necessary to allow the United States to effectively compete in the global competition for economic development, such as in artificial intelligence and creating manufacturing and industrial jobs.
quotes.concern: Uniform rules may undercut U.S. competitiveness by slowing large load connections.

quotes.text: In lieu of requiring standard large load interconnection procedures, and in light of the concerns expressed above, the Commission should allow MISO, as well as other industry participants, to continue focusing attention on efficiently interconnecting large loads pursuant to processes that reflect the unique circumstances of their members, states, stakeholders, and transmission systems.
quotes.concern: MISO asks that the Commission refrain from mandating a uniform process and let RTOs keep their regional approaches.

quotes.text: Alternatively, the Commission could refine the principles started by DOE and issue a policy statement to guide the industry processes to be used for large load interconnections.  This would enable Transmission Providers to craft unique solutions suited for their regions guided by Commission principles.
quotes.concern: A policy statement with guiding principles is a less disruptive alternative to a formal rule.

quotes.text: To the extent the Commission believes it must pursue standard large load interconnection procedures or another rule mandate through a formal rulemaking process, it should extend the "Independent Entity Variation" principle to any final rule.
quotes.concern: If a rule is issued, RTOs should get the same flexibility they have under generator interconnection rules.

quotes.text: under MISO's existing processes, since the 2022 MTEP cycle, MISO has approved approximately 17 GW of large load additions.
quotes.concern: MISO's track record shows its current process is working without federal standardization.

quotes.text: utilizing a generator interconnection process as a template that historically has experienced delays and is unlikely to provide the speed to power needed by large load customers
quotes.concern: The generator queue model has a poor track record and is a poor template for load.

quotes.text: only about 13% of the total capacity entering interconnection queues nationwide ultimately achieves commercial operation.  Withdrawals of speculative interconnection requests frequently require restudies, particularly for later-stage withdrawals, which in turn could delay completion of studies and ultimately delay bringing viable generation proposals online.
quotes.concern: High withdrawal rates in generator queues cause restudy delays that would carry over to load queues.

quotes.text: there is no evidence of undue discrimination of loads, large or small, by load serving utilities.  Unlike generators, individual load additions can site where they want and are not assigned costs for their impacts to the transmission system at a wholesale level.
quotes.concern: The open-access rationale that justified generator interconnection rules does not apply to load.

quotes.text: Creating a parallel set of provisions at the federal level will likely result in confusion and may create opportunities for forum shopping.
quotes.concern: Federal load interconnection rules would conflict with existing state-level retail oversight.

quotes.text: a universal definition of "large load" should not be based solely on size.  Instead, MISO recommends a hybrid approach to determining what constitutes a "large load."  The hybrid approach could consist of: (i) NERC registration or NERC Reliability Standards development; and (ii) allowing ISO/RTOs to determine specific minimum thresholds to support market registration and application processes that fit the unique needs of their footprints.
quotes.concern: A size-only national threshold ignores regional grid differences; MISO prefers a NERC plus RTO hybrid definition.

quotes.text: This principle would call for MISO allocating such costs directly to the large load customer.  MISO has concerns with this approach as it may reduce the options and flexibility that exist today between the LSE, large load customer, and possibly the retail jurisdiction, to develop creative approaches for cost recovery to more readily facilitate siting of new large load additions.
quotes.concern: Mandatory direct cost assignment to large load customers limits the flexible cost-recovery arrangements that help attract investment.

quotes.text: bulk power system owners, operators, and users must register with NERC to ensure compliance with mandatory reliability standards.  This includes generators.  However, loads are not currently required to register with NERC.  Therefore, there would currently be no responsibility or accountability for loads that build interconnection facilities or network upgrades.  Consequently, MISO views this principle as impractical.
quotes.concern: The option-to-build principle is unworkable for loads because loads lack NERC registration and transmission expertise.

quotes.text: if the Commission decides to move forward with standard large load interconnection procedures, it should also consider how to mitigate the potential increase in congestion along the seam(s) that could result from the interconnection of large loads.
quotes.concern: Standardized interconnection procedures could worsen cross-border congestion between MISO and neighboring RTOs.

bins.name: Regional Process Over Uniform Rule
bins.description: MISO argues that standardized study procedures modeled on the generator interconnection queue would introduce the same delays that plagued Order No. 2003 and subsequent reforms. MISO's existing processes already approved about 17 GW of large loads since 2022 and allow requests to be studied as they arrive rather than queued in cycles.

bins.name: RTO Flexibility and Independent Entity Variation
bins.description: MISO supports preserving regional flexibility by asking the Commission to let RTOs continue their own processes, or at most issue a policy statement rather than a binding rule. If a rule is unavoidable, MISO asks that the Independent Entity Variation be extended to allow significant regional deviations.

bins.name: Federal vs. State Jurisdictional Limits
bins.description: MISO raises concerns that large load interconnection may involve retail electricity sales outside FERC's authority, and that federal rules could conflict with existing state commissions that already oversee load-serving entities. MISO does not deny FERC has some authority, but urges caution about displacing state and member roles.

bins.name: Cost Assignment and Flexibility
bins.description: MISO opposes requiring 100% direct cost assignment to large load customers, arguing this reduces the creative cost-recovery arrangements between load-serving entities and large loads that currently help bring projects online. MISO also notes that the generator queue's speculative-withdrawal problem would carry cost implications into any load queue.

bins.name: Large Load Definition and Threshold
bins.description: MISO opposes a purely size-based national threshold for defining large load, noting that grid impacts vary by location and density. MISO recommends a hybrid approach combining NERC registration categories with RTO-specific minimum thresholds.

bins.name: Speculative Requests and Queue Discipline
bins.description: MISO acknowledges that if a load queue were created, deposits and withdrawal penalties would be needed to deter speculative entries, consistent with what Order No. 2023 imposed on generators. However, MISO prefers its existing pre-screening approach over formal queue discipline.

bins.name: Upgrade Cost Assignment to Large Loads
bins.description: MISO is concerned that mandatory wholesale-level cost assignment would remove flexibility currently used by load-serving entities and states to negotiate cost recovery, potentially discouraging large load siting.

bins.name: MISO Regional Track Record
bins.description: MISO documents its existing processes, including the EPR, ERAS, and LRTP, and notes approximately 17 GW of large load approvals since 2022 and a growing EPR pipeline, to show that regional processes are already delivering results.

bins.name: MISO-PJM Seam Congestion Risk
bins.description: MISO notes that large load growth could worsen congestion on the MISO-PJM seam and affect the Market-to-Market coordination process if a uniform interconnection rule does not also address cross-border flow management.

bins.name: MISO-SPP Seam Congestion Risk
bins.description: MISO raises the same seam-congestion concern for the MISO-SPP border, noting the Market-to-Market process could be strained by a rapid influx of large loads without corresponding transmission or generation.

bins.name: NERC Registration Gap for Loads
bins.description: MISO flags that loads, unlike generators, are not currently required to register with NERC, making the option-to-build provision and other obligations drawn from generator rules impractical to extend to load customers without first resolving the registration gap.

bins.name: Cross-Border Seam Congestion
bins.description: MISO warns that large load growth could increase congestion at the MISO-PJM and MISO-SPP seams, straining the Market-to-Market coordination process and raising costs, an issue any standardized interconnection rule should address.
